title: Operators
summary: Perform calculations and compare values using JavaScript operators

sections.title: Operators Are Math Tools
sections.content: Operators are symbols that perform operations on values, just like buttons on a calculator. The most familiar ones are arithmetic operators: + (add), - (subtract), * (multiply), and / (divide). There is also % (modulus) which gives the remainder of division — think of it as what is left over after splitting a pizza evenly. Beyond math, JavaScript has comparison operators (like > and ===) that check relationships between values and return true or false.

sections.title: Arithmetic and comparison operators in action
sections.content: Performs basic calculations and comparisons using common operators

sections.title: The Difference Between =, ==, and ===
sections.content: A single = is assignment — it puts a value into a variable. Two == is loose equality — it checks if values are equal after converting types (like '5' == 5 is true). Three === is strict equality — it checks value AND type without conversion ('5' === 5 is false). Always use === unless you have a specific reason to allow type conversion. This prevents subtle bugs where JavaScript silently changes types in unexpected ways.

sections.title: Confusing assignment with comparison
sections.content: The #1 mistake beginners make is using = instead of === inside conditions. if (score = 100) does not check if score equals 100 — it assigns 100 to score, and the condition is always true. Wrong: if (score = 100). Correct: if (score === 100). This is such a common bug that many linters flag single = inside if statements.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: A coffee shop calculates bills. A customer buys 3 coffees at $4 each and 2 muffins at $3.50 each. Write code to compute the subtotal, apply a 10% discount if the subtotal is over $15, and print the final amount. Use variables, arithmetic, and comparison operators.
hints: How do you calculate a percentage of a number in JavaScript?
Which operator checks if one value is greater than another?
Should the condition use =, ==, or === to check the subtotal?
